title: Mesa-Optimization is Destroying Education
gist: American education's core metrics (GPAs, SAT scores, remedial-placement data) have been hollowed out by decades of institutional 'mesa-optimization' — administrators, teachers, and testmakers gaming proxies instead of teaching — and the same AI alignment framework that names the disease points to a fix: AI-native, auditable assessment systems (modeled loosely on Alpha School) that move the gaming up a level, where it becomes visible and correctable.

themes.theme: Campbell's Law and the Mesa-Optimization Framework

insights.claim: Donald T. Campbell's 1976 principle (Campbell's Law) holds that any quantitative indicator used for social decision-making inevitably becomes a target for manipulation, and as pressure to hit that target grows, the measure loses its connection to the reality it was meant to track.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Use as a general diagnostic for any KPI-driven system (schools, companies, regulators) to spot when the metric has decoupled from the underlying reality it was designed to capture.
insights.quote: will distort and corrupt the social processes it is intended to monitor
insights.locator: In 1976 the social scientist and cyberneticist Donald T. Campbell

insights.claim: Mesa-optimization, a term from the 2019 AI alignment paper 'Risks from Learned Optimization in Advanced Machine Learning Systems' co-authored by researchers from the Machine Intelligence Research Institute (MIRI), describes how a training process (the 'base optimizer') can produce a model that runs its own internal search toward a 'mesa-objective' that merely correlates with, but is not identical to, the original training goal.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Gives a rigorous vocabulary for diagnosing why any large KPI-optimized organization (schools, companies, agencies) drifts from its stated mission over time — the same lens applies to internal incentive systems in firms and institutions.
insights.quote: where a meta-optimizer sits one level above the system it tunes, a mesa-optimizer sits one level below
insights.locator: How Signal Gets Distorted

insights.claim: The author's central analogy is that natural selection (the base process optimizing for reproduction) produced humans who independently pursue behaviors unrelated or even opposed to reproduction, such as celibacy, contraception, and adoption — illustrating how any large-scale optimization process can spawn agents whose goals diverge from the original objective.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Reframe any institution, market, or AI training run you're evaluating by asking what 'mesa-objective' its agents have actually adopted versus what it was designed to optimize for.
insights.quote: Optimizing for reproduction, the “base process” produced a new class of agents—humans—who possess independent goals unrelated to reproduction itself.
insights.locator: The process of natural selection

insights.claim: In any large human institution there is an identifiable base objective (what it was designed to do) and a subsequent web of downstream mesa-objectives that emerge from incentive and survival pressure on the people operating inside it; Campbell's Law explains how, left unchecked, those mesa-objectives corrupt the base objective itself.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A general checklist for auditing any KPI-driven organization: identify the base objective, then map the mesa-objectives incentives have actually produced.
insights.locator: In any large human institution we can identify a base objective

insights.claim: Campbell's original 1970s examples of metric corruption include prosecutors misusing plea bargains to pin unsolved crimes on defendants in exchange for lighter sentences (inflating case-clearance rates) and U.S. commanders in the Vietnam War inflating enemy-killed-in-action counts, including counting civilian dead as KIA, to hit body-count targets.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Historical proof that any hard, career-linked KPI gets gamed, not just soft ones like grades — a caution against trusting official statistics from any performance-incentivized bureaucracy.
insights.locator: Campbell described a few contemporary phenomena

insights.claim: A 2025 UC San Diego report found that roughly one in eight incoming freshmen tested below high school level in math, with nearly 12 percent of the entering class enrolling in remedial courses; the author treats UCSD's prestige as evidence this is a representative signal for the broader university ecosystem, not an isolated case.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A leading-indicator data point for the scale of credential-versus-competence divergence entering the labor pipeline from elite-adjacent universities.
insights.quote: roughly one in eight incoming freshmen tested below high school level in math, with nearly 12 percent of the entering class enrolling in remedial courses
insights.locator: At UC San Diego, a 2025 report

insights.claim: At UC San Diego, students placed in the lowest remedial math track had an average high school math GPA of an A-minus, showing the high school GPA signal had become almost completely decoupled from actual math competence.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Quantifies exactly how corrupted the GPA signal has become — an A-minus transcript predicts almost nothing about remedial-level math ability.
insights.quote: the average high school math GPA among those placed in the lowest remedial track stood at an A-minus
insights.locator: At UC San Diego, a 2025 report

insights.claim: UCSD's remedial-math surge followed its decision to drop standardized testing as an admissions/assessment measure, removing one of the few tools that had counteracted high-school grade inflation.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Direct causal link between dropping standardized testing and the erosion of a competence signal — relevant to any institution debating test-optional admissions policy.
insights.locator: In UCSD’s case, these results came after standardized testing was dropped

insights.claim: College GPAs have risen roughly a tenth of a point per decade, and the A grade is now the single most commonly awarded grade at close to 43 percent of all grades given, versus far rarer a generation earlier.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Quantifies grade inflation as a slow, structural, multi-decade trend rather than a recent AI-era phenomenon — undercuts the narrative that LLMs alone caused the credential collapse.
insights.quote: the A has become the most commonly awarded grade—close to 43 percent of the total
insights.locator: College GPAs began rising

insights.claim: Between 2010 and 2021, average U.S. high school GPA climbed from 3.17 to 3.36 even as average ACT scores fell to their lowest point of the decade — a direct divergence between self-reported (school-graded) achievement and independently tested achievement.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A clean, dated statistic showing grade inflation and actual skill decline moving in opposite directions — a leading indicator that GPA-based signals for college admissions and hiring are increasingly unreliable.
insights.quote: the average high school GPA climbed from 3.17 to 3.36 even as average ACT scores fell to their lowest point of the decade
insights.locator: Between 2010 and 2021

insights.claim: In 1995 the College Board 'recentered' the SAT scale to restore a 500 national average per section, which instantly added roughly eighty points to typical verbal scores and twenty points to math scores — a direct, administrative inflation of the test itself, later compounded by widespread gaming of disability accommodations (like extended time) and the spread of test-optional admissions, with the University of California system dropping the SAT/ACT entirely.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Documents multiple, compounding mechanisms of test-score inflation across 30 years — useful context for anyone relying on historical SAT/ACT trend data as a proxy for cohort ability.
insights.quote: instantly adding roughly eighty points to the typical verbal score and twenty to the math
insights.locator: In 1995, the College Board “recentered” the SAT scale

insights.claim: Over a third of undergraduates at elite academic institutions now have disability accommodations, which the author frames as evidence of an expensive, humiliating 'signaling arms race' born from the collapse of honest measurement.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A striking statistic for anyone assessing the credibility of elite-university credentials or disability-accommodation policy as currently structured.
insights.quote: over a third of undergraduates at elite academic institutions have disability accommodations
insights.locator: Even a short period of measured decline

insights.claim: Hannah Arendt argued in 1958 that America's education crisis was not fundamentally an education problem but a fragment of a broader modern crisis of authority, in which adults no longer felt entitled to present the world to the young and take responsibility for it.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Reframes today's AI-in-education debate as fundamentally about restoring adult judgment and accountability, not just better tools or metrics.
insights.quote: Nowhere have the education problems of a mass society become so acute, and nowhere else have the most modern theories in the realm of pedagogy been so uncritically and slavishly accepted.
insights.locator: In 1958 Hannah Arendt argued

insights.claim: Per Arendt, progressive reformers posited an autonomous 'child's world' that should be largely self-governed by children, with adults reduced to helpers — a theory that relieved adults of the need to exercise judgment and authority over students.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Identifies the ideological root of decades of grade-softening and standard-lowering as a deliberate abdication of adult authority, not a technical failure.
insights.quote: child’s world and a society formed among children that are autonomous and must insofar as possible be left to them to govern. Adults are only there to help with this government.
insights.locator: Reformers, she claimed, posited

insights.claim: The expansion of federally funded mass education (GI Bill of 1944, Higher Education Act of 1965) coincided with the dominance of John Dewey-style 'adjustment learning' theory, which held adults had no right to compel instruction — leaving institutions helpless to impose standards on students just as enrollment scaled dramatically.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Explains the structural origin of grade inflation as a collision between mass-scale institutions and an ideology that discouraged rigor at exactly the wrong moment.
insights.locator: The GI Bill of 1944 then sent millions of returning veterans

insights.claim: Education was historically an elite pursuit: Harvard (founded 1636) trained clergy and gentry in a classical curriculum, and as late as 1900 only about 3.5 percent of college-age Americans were enrolled in higher education, rising to only about 7 percent by 1940.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Baseline context showing mass higher education is a mid-20th-century phenomenon, useful for framing how quickly institutional incentives shifted once enrollment scaled.
insights.quote: as late as 1900 only about 3.5 percent of college-age Americans were enrolled in higher education at all
insights.locator: Historically, education was a virtuous pursuit

insights.claim: By the late 1980s, university administrators had developed a new vocabulary around 'success rates' as if retention/completion metrics were the base objective of education, rather than a proxy for it — a linguistic marker of mesa-optimization taking hold institutionally.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A dateable inflection point where administrative language itself reveals the proxy (retention/completion) had replaced the goal (actual learning) inside university management.
insights.locator: By the late 1980s a new vocabulary had taken hold among administrators

insights.claim: Financial dependence on steady tuition revenue, combined with social/parental pressure and a later focus on 'student satisfaction,' systematically pushed universities from pedagogy toward customer-service objectives, while high schools faced parallel pressure to inflate GPAs to boost college admission rates since colleges gatekeep 'good jobs.'
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Maps the specific financial incentive chain (tuition dependence → satisfaction focus → grade inflation) that any analysis of higher-ed business models should account for.
insights.locator: The need for steady tuition revenues

insights.claim: As GPAs and SAT scores became unreliable, employers shifted to costlier, higher-variance signals — number and prestige of internships, and visible project work such as public GitHub repositories — to discern candidate quality.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Explains why internships and portfolio work have become de facto hiring gates: they are a rational market response to credential inflation, not arbitrary employer preference.
insights.quote: How many internships did the student have and where? What actual work did they publish on their GitHub or in other public channels?
insights.locator: In response, employers struggling to discern high-quality candidates

insights.claim: When a primary, cheap signal (like GPA) collapses, the market inevitably turns to expensive, high-variance alternative signals — admissions offices likewise turn to essays, extracurriculars, and philanthropy, which are themselves strongly socioeconomically correlated.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A general signaling-theory lens: watch for expensive, effort-intensive proxies emerging wherever a cheap credential has been debased — often a sign the underlying institution has lost measurement integrity, and often regressive since costly signals favor the wealthy.
insights.quote: These costly signals replace the original, cheap signals that have stopped carrying information.
insights.locator: The only reason these became necessary signals

themes.theme: Alpha School as a Case Study

insights.claim: Alpha School, an Austin-based private school, runs instruction and assessment primarily through a proprietary AI system that generates endlessly varied assessments to prevent gaming and requires 90 percent accuracy before a student can unlock new material, evaluating answers uniformly across classrooms and districts.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A concrete, operating example of an AI-native assessment model that removes teacher-level grading discretion — a template worth tracking as a potential ed-tech/policy investment thesis.
insights.quote: require 90 percent accuracy before unlocking new material at the student’s own pace
insights.locator: Alpha School is an Austin-based private school

insights.claim: In 2025 it was revealed that Alpha School's 'guides' (who replaced teachers) mostly consist of remote workers from the Philippines, undercutting the parental trust the school's AI-first model depends on.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A concrete governance red flag for evaluating any AI-education venture that claims to preserve human mentorship — check who is actually behind the 'guide' role and their qualifications/proximity to students.
insights.quote: In 2025 they were revealed to mostly consist of remote workers from the Philippines, which is something no one would want for their child.
insights.locator: Under this AI-first system, teachers are replaced by “guides”

insights.claim: The author argues Alpha School solved the measurement/gaming problem but simultaneously completed the abdication of adult responsibility that Arendt warned about, since no adult in the room stands behind any final judgment of the student.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A key caution for anyone touting AI-native schools as a full solution: fixing the metric-gaming problem does not automatically restore the human-authority function education also needs.
insights.locator: In this way Alpha solved the measurement problem

insights.claim: Alpha students perform extremely well on nationally used Measures of Academic Progress (MAP) tests, but the author flags that relying solely on this one metric risks the same Campbell's Law dynamics all over again, and it will take years to see how these students actually perform in higher education and the workforce.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A built-in caveat against over-trusting even the newest AI-native assessment regime without long-horizon outcome tracking — apply the same skepticism to any single-metric success claim.
insights.quote: Alpha students perform extremely well on Measures of Academic Progress (MAP) tests that are used nationally to evaluate student progress.
insights.locator: Alpha students perform extremely well

themes.theme: AI as Policy Solution: Design Principles

insights.claim: The author's core contrarian argument is that AI assessment systems are more resistant than human institutions to Campbell's Law-style gaming, precisely because when gaming occurs it moves up to the administrator level (choosing difficulty settings, subject coverage, competency thresholds), where it becomes visible and auditable to parents, teachers, and other stakeholders — unlike a teacher quietly inflating grades behind a closed door.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: The key investable thesis of the piece: AI-native assessment isn't gaming-proof, but it relocates gaming to a visible, auditable layer — a design principle applicable to any institution (finance, healthcare, government) trying to build gaming-resistant metrics.
insights.quote: AI is more resistant, precisely because the gaming moves up a level, and at that level it becomes more visible to parents, teachers, and other interested parties.
insights.locator: The relevant question, however

insights.claim: The author proposes state-mandated 'model cards' for AI assessment systems that publish difficulty calibrations, the underlying standard teaching corpus, and expected score distributions for median students at each level, so that when a district picks an easy setting, everyone can see that choice.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A concrete policy/regulatory mechanism (model-card-style disclosure) that could become a template for AI-education regulation — worth tracking for ed-tech policy and compliance plays.
insights.quote: State-mandated model cards could publish difficulty calibrations, the underlying standard teaching corpus, and expected score distributions for median students at each level.
insights.locator: However, unlike a teacher inflating grades behind a closed door

insights.claim: Common, auditable AI assessment standards would enable upward mobility that the current system blocks: a student in a low-rigor district who aces every assessment could have parents advocate for a difficulty upgrade specific to that child, without switching schools, because capability becomes visible against a shared standard rather than hidden behind an uninterpretable local grade.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A specific second-order social-mobility benefit of standardized AI assessment — relevant to anyone evaluating ed-tech's equity impact, not just its efficiency impact.
insights.quote: A student in a low-rigor district who aces every assessment can have parents advocate for a difficulty upgrade for that child specifically, without changing schools.
insights.locator: This transparency also enables upward mobility

insights.claim: Continuous AI micro-assessment generates a time series for each student, eliminating a struggling teacher's ability to claim they simply inherited weak students, and instead allows administrators to distinguish teachers who consistently produce upward learning trajectories from those whose students stagnate or decline, regardless of starting point.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A specific mechanism for teacher accountability that removes a commonly used excuse — relevant to anyone designing or evaluating teacher-performance measurement systems.
insights.quote: Under the current system, a struggling teacher can plausibly claim that they inherited weak students from the prior grade. This excuse disappears when we have continuous assessment data.
insights.locator: Under the current system, a struggling teacher

insights.claim: The author insists AI assessment cannot be bolted onto existing testing regimes or added as a learning-management-system feature; it must be a full strategic replacement of existing assessment infrastructure implemented at the state level, or the current system's distortions will simply absorb and neutralize it.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A sharp implementation warning for ed-tech vendors and policymakers: incremental 'AI integration' products sold to legacy institutions are structurally unlikely to fix the underlying signal problem — full infrastructure replacement is the load-bearing requirement.
insights.quote: The current system’s distortions would simply absorb and neutralize it.
insights.locator: What Alpha School gets right

insights.claim: The author predicts real implementation of gaming-resistant AI assessment will require a state with unusual political cover — either a governor with political capital to spend or a jurisdiction desperate enough because it is far behind in the current system — and it might not happen in the U.S. first.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A concrete forecast for where to watch for first-mover policy action: distressed or politically unconstrained jurisdictions, potentially outside the U.S., rather than flagship wealthy districts.
insights.quote: It requires a state with unusual political cover—a governor with political capital to spend, or a jurisdiction desperate enough to try something because they are so far behind in the current regime. It might not even happen in the U.S. first.
insights.locator: Planners need to accept that honest measurement

insights.claim: The author predicts that honestly measuring student competence via AI assessment will cause GPAs to crater and failure rates to spike in the short term, with that political and reputational cost falling entirely on whichever administration implements it, even though the long-term benefit of restored signal diffuses across the whole economy.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Explains why no institution has voluntarily implemented honest AI assessment yet: the cost/benefit is asymmetric, with concentrated short-term political pain and diffuse long-term economic gain — a classic reform-adoption barrier to watch for.
insights.quote: GPAs will crater and failure rates will spike.
insights.locator: Planners need to accept that honest measurement

insights.claim: If AI takes over assessment, the author predicts teachers' roles will shift toward observation, mentorship, and instilling academic discipline — resembling pre-industrial 'schoolhouse' models — but warns that interacting with a sycophantic AI model can be damaging for a growing mind, making human feedback more necessary, not less, to prevent 'AI psychosis.'
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: Flags a specific risk (AI sycophancy/'AI psychosis' in children) that any AI-education deployment plan needs to design around, not just the measurement layer.
insights.quote: Interacting with a sycophantic AI model can be damaging for a growing mind; human feedback becomes more necessary to promote the habits of mental discipline and prevent AI psychosis.
insights.locator: The natural implementing body is the state

insights.claim: The author argues the mesa-optimization/Campbell's Law framework used to diagnose education can be applied to other intractable systems corrupted by thousands of small, incentive-driven distortions, explicitly naming community safety, healthcare, and 'purportedly unbiased' information systems like journalism as candidates for the same measurement audit.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A direct pointer to where to next apply this same diagnostic lens for investment or policy research — healthcare quality metrics, crime statistics, and journalism 'objectivity' metrics are flagged as likely to show the same Campbell's Law corruption pattern.
insights.quote: We can apply this framework to some of our other intractable problems like community safety, healthcare, and purportedly unbiased information systems like journalism.
insights.locator: We set up many human systems with clear, prosocial objectives

insights.claim: The author's closing, contrarian-to-AI-hype thesis is that AI's most valuable near-term application is not sci-fi-level capability or existential risk but restoring honest, gaming-resistant measurement to mundane, intractable institutional problems — because human systems do not self-correct toward truth without a measurement regime that cannot be gamed.
insights.speaker: Matt Duffy
insights.so_what: A reframing of where AI's economic value actually accrues — favor AI applications targeted at institutional measurement/auditing over speculative frontier-capability narratives when assessing near-term impact.
insights.quote: Human systems do not move back toward truth on their own; they need, at minimum, a measurement regime that cannot be gamed. AI gives us the first chance in ages to restore the base objective of education and make measurements honest again.
insights.locator: Too much present-day hype around AI
